title: ExtractSignalsBin passes Go-owned slice into cgo without runtime.KeepAlive — use-after-free across the FFI boundary
proof: The mechanical observations are all true and personally re-verified: line 799 derives `dataPtr = (*C.uint8_t)(unsafe.Pointer(&data[0]))`; lines 806-816 pass it into the synchronous cgo trampoline call_extract_signals_bin (signature ffi.go:92-100, which reads `uint8_t *data` C-side); `grep -n KeepAlive ffi.go` returns ONLY 505/632/689-692/763-767, so there is NO runtime.KeepAlive(data) anywhere in 782-835; the sibling ExtractSignalsBinary (line 606) uses the identical &data[0] shape (line 621) and DOES guard with runtime.KeepAlive(data) at line 632; the BuildFrameBin rationale (lines 683-688) documents the inliner/GC concern verbatim; callers client.go:485 and client.go:1137 pass Go-owned []byte(data). HOWEVER the claimed CONSEQUENCE — a reachable use-after-free — is REFUTED. cgo's pointer-passing contract (go doc cmd/cgo, "Passing pointers") guarantees that Go memory referenced by a typed-pointer call ARGUMENT stays valid (not freed, not moved) for the duration of the call. dataPtr is a genuine *C.uint8_t (not a uintptr), passed as a live argument to call_extract_signals_bin; the compiler's liveness analysis cannot mark data's backing array dead before the call that consumes the pointer returns, and a live pointer into the array keeps the whole array reachable to a non-moving GC. So there is no window in which the array is unreachable during the C read. Confirmed via grep that NO runtime.SetFinalizer exists anywhere in go/aletheia/ — and runtime.KeepAlive's only load-bearing effect is delaying premature finalizer execution, which cannot occur here. The siblings' KeepAlive calls are therefore defensive convention (the comment itself says "Defensive against future inliner / GC"), not load-bearing UAF guards. The finding's own hedge ("GC-timing-dependent ... not a deterministic input reproducer") is an admission that no concrete trigger exists; under the proof bar (refute unless the UAF trigger is concretely reachable) the safety claim does not hold.
suggested_fix: Recharacterize as an intra-binding consistency/convention gap, not a use-after-free. ExtractSignalsBin is the lone binary-payload method in ffi.go that omits the defensive runtime.KeepAlive(data) convention every sibling (ExtractSignalsBinary:632, BuildFrameBin:689-692, UpdateFrameBin:763-767, SendFrameBinary:505) follows. Adding `runtime.KeepAlive(data)` immediately after the C.call_extract_signals_bin(...) call returns (after line 816, before the status check) is still the correct change — it restores file-wide convention consistency and future-proofs against any later refactor — but it closes a style/convention gap, not a live memory-safety hazard. KeepAlive is Go-specific with no cross-binding analogue, so this is single-binding.

title: within() converts ms→µs via duration_cast with no overflow guard → signed-integer-overflow UB on large within_ms (reachable from untrusted YAML)
proof: Re-read and verified. check.hpp:142-144 (SettlesBuilder::within) and 221-223 (ThenCondition::within) guard ONLY `if (ms.count() < 0) throw`, then call `auto us = std::chrono::duration_cast<Timestamp>(ms);`. types.hpp:233 `using Timestamp = std::chrono::microseconds;` — int64 rep. duration_cast<microseconds>(milliseconds) computes `count * 1000` in common_type (int64/intmax_t = int64); the `<0` guard does NOT catch large-positive overflow, so count > INT64_MAX/1000 (~9.223e15) makes `count*1000` overflow signed int64 = UB, occurring at the cast before any downstream use (ltl::within/always_within at ltl.hpp:219-225 just store the value, no guard). TRIGGER reachable from untrusted input: yaml.cpp:124 `auto ms = std::chrono::milliseconds{get_int(entry,"within_ms",ctx(name))}; ... .within(ms)` and yaml.cpp:147/169-181 (when/then). get_int (yaml.cpp:52-61) returns `child.as<std::int64_t>()` with NO upper cap. A YAML with `within_ms: 9300000000000000` (accepted by get_int, passes <0) → 9.3e15*1000 ≈ 9.3e18 > INT64_MAX → signed overflow UB. PEER GUARDS verified present: Go check.go:200-201 and :344-345 `if timeMs > math.MaxInt64/usPerMillisecond { return ..."overflows microsecond conversion" }`; Rust check.rs:46 `ms.checked_mul(US_PER_MS).ok_or_else(..."overflows microsecond conversion")`. C++ has no equivalent.
suggested_fix: Mirror Go/Rust: before duration_cast in BOTH SettlesBuilder::within and ThenCondition::within, guard the multiply, e.g. `if (ms.count() > std::chrono::milliseconds::rep{std::numeric_limits<Timestamp::rep>::max() / 1000}) throw std::invalid_argument("time " + std::to_string(ms.count()) + " ms overflows microsecond conversion");`. Add a unit test `within(milliseconds{INT64_MAX/1000 + 1})` asserting throw plus a YAML test with an oversize within_ms.

title: LazyIndex caches positional indices into a public mutable vector with no invalidation → stale index reaches unchecked vector::operator[] (out-of-bounds → UB)
proof: Re-read and verified. dbc.cpp:89-100 signal_by_name builds the cache once via signal_index_cache.ensure(...) mapping name→positional index i, then `return &signals[*idx];` (unchecked operator[]); identical at dbc.cpp:106-123 (message_by_id, `return &messages[*idx];`) and dbc.cpp:125-136 (message_by_name). LazyIndex (dbc.hpp:34-39) `ensure` is a permanent no-op after first call (`if (cache_) return;`) and exposes NO invalidate() — confirmed: grep found no `invalidate` anywhere in cpp/ outside vendored deps. The indexed members are public mutable aggregate fields: dbc.hpp:113 `std::vector<DbcSignal> signals;`, dbc.hpp:371 `std::vector<DbcMessage> messages;`. find() returns std::optional<std::size_t>; std::vector::operator[] with index >= size() is UB (no bounds check). TRIGGER concretely constructible via the public API: `DbcDefinition d=...; d.message_by_id(idA); // populates id_index_cache e.g. {keyA:0,keyB:1}; d.messages.erase(d.messages.begin()); // size()==1, cache still maps keyB→1; d.message_by_id(idB); // find()→idx 1; &messages[1] with size 1 → OOB read = UB`. Same for signal_by_name after shrinking signals. No documentation forbids post-construction mutation (dbc.hpp:24 only claims const-safe population). NOTE: no current in-repo code triggers it (parse path at json_parse.cpp:394/646 and excel.cpp:576/645 fully builds vectors before any query), so the trigger requires unusual user mutation-after-query — but the public mutable fields permit it and the UB sink is real. C++-only: peers do not cache (Rust dbc.rs:1037/1048/1056 linear-scan `.iter().find`; Python _queries.py:107/120/133 linear-scan), so they have no stale-index hazard.
suggested_fix: Pick one: (a) make signals/messages private with mutating accessors that call a new LazyIndex::invalidate() (reset cache_ to nullopt) on each mutation; (b) drop the cache and linear-scan like the peers and like the sibling helpers always_present_signals/multiplexor_names in the same file; or (c) document DbcDefinition/DbcMessage as immutable-after-construction AND add a defensive `if (*idx >= signals.size()) return nullptr;` so a stale index can never reach operator[]. Add a test: query, shrink the vector, re-query, assert no OOB.

title: `never_exceeds(v)` compiles to `G(s < v)` (strict) and flags the in-bound value `s == v` as a violation, contradicting the codebase's own definition of "exceeds" (= strict `>`); the dual `never_below` uses the correct inclusive boundary
proof:
RE-VERIFIED. The decisive proof is an internal contradiction within python/aletheia/checks.py, requiring no external docs:
(1) The same check vocabulary defines "exceeds" as STRICT greater-than: checks.py:246-248 `def exceeds(self, value): ... pred = Signal(self._then_name).greater_than(value)` and checks.py:282-284 `def exceeds(self, value): ... pred = Signal(self._name).greater_than(value)`. So in this DSL, "signal exceeds v" ≡ `s > v`.
(2) Therefore "never exceeds v" MUST be the negation `G(¬(s > v))` = `G(s <= v)`.
(3) But checks.py:150-152 gives `def never_exceeds(self, value): prop = Signal(self._name).less_than(value).always()` (docstring `— G(s < v)`). `G(s < v)` is the negation of `s >= v` ("at least v"), NOT of `s > v` ("exceeds v"). So `never_exceeds(v)` reports a violation when `s == v`, even though `s == v` does not exceed `v`.
(4) Corroboration, same file: the dual `never_below` IS built by the correct negation rule — checks.py:159-161 `Signal(self._name).greater_than_or_equal(value).always()` (`¬(s < v)` ≡ `s >= v`), and `stays_between` (checks.py:168-171) is fully closed `[lo, hi]`. `never_exceeds` is the lone outlier producing a half-open band, with no comment justifying the asymmetry — the tell of a bug, not a design choice.
All four bindings reproduce the identical strict-`<` defect (RE-READ each):
- go/aletheia/check.go:84 `Always{Inner: Atomic{Predicate: LessThan{...}}}` (vs NeverBelow:98 `GreaterThanOrEqual`);
- cpp/include/aletheia/check.hpp:165 `ltl::less_than(SignalName{name_}, value)` (vs never_below:171 `ltl::at_least`);
- rust/src/check.rs:142 `Predicate::LessThan {...}` (vs never_below:153 `GreaterThanOrEqual`).
The bug is locked in by tests that pin it: rust/src/check.rs:469-481 `never_exceeds_is_always_less_than` asserts `Predicate::LessThan` / `"< 120"` (while :483 `never_below_is_always_gte` pins the inclusive form — the same asymmetry).
Downstream propagation (NOT authority — the bug leaked into docs): TUTORIAL.md:219 `never_exceeds(220) # G(speed < 220)`, :308. Secondary corroboration of intended English meaning: PITCH.md:14 "Speed never exceeds 220 km/h" (220 itself does not exceed 220).
Fix verified clean: `less_than_or_equal` exists as a distinct DSL predicate (python/aletheia/dsl.py:223 + `LessThanOrEqualPredicate` wire type types.py:242), so the swap `less_than`→`less_than_or_equal` is a clean one-line change per binding. (Dropped the round-1 `min_exceeds_max` strict-vs-inclusive citation — not re-verified and not load-bearing given the internal `exceeds` builder.)
suggested_fix:
Change the predicate from strict `<` to inclusive `<=` in all four bindings (the negation of "exceeds" = `>`):
- Python checks.py:151 `Signal(self._name).less_than(value)` → `.less_than_or_equal(value)`; update docstring :151 and condition_desc :156 `f"< {value}"` → `f"<= {value}"`.
- Go check.go:84 `LessThan{...}` → `LessThanOrEqual{...}`; comment :75 `G(signal < value)` → `G(signal <= value)`; desc :87 `"< %g"` → `"<= %g"`.
- C++ check.hpp:165 `ltl::less_than(...)` → `ltl::at_most(...)`; desc :167 `"< "` → `"<= "`.
- Rust check.rs:142 `Predicate::LessThan` → `Predicate::LessThanOrEqual`; doc :138 `G(signal < value)` → `<=`; desc :146 `"< {}"` → `"<= {}"`.
Retarget the tests that pin the wrong behavior (do NOT keep `< 120`): rust/src/check.rs:469 `never_exceeds_is_always_less_than` → assert `LessThanOrEqual` / `"<= 120"`; the Go/C++/Python equivalents likewise. Add a boundary test asserting `s == v` does NOT violate `never_exceeds(v)`. Update docs: TUTORIAL.md:219 `# G(speed < 220)` → `# G(speed <= 220)`.

title: DBC message decode skips CAN-id range, DLC byte-count, startBit and bit-length validation that the same normalizer applies to other numeric fields (and Go/C++ enforce)
proof: Re-read dbc_normalize.py: normalize_dbc (489) builds each message via `msg_dict: dict[str, object] = dict(m)` then `messages.append(cast("DBCMessage", msg_dict))` (521-523) with NO check of id/dlc; normalize_signal (114-121) only parse_rationals factor/offset/min/max then `return cast("DBCSignal", sig)` — no startBit/length check. The 'TypedDict can't validate' defense fails because the SAME file validates other decoded numerics: _normalize_var_type (145-148) raises ProtocolError unless varType in (0,1,2); _normalize_value_entry (172-174) raises unless value is non-negative int. Peer enforcement re-verified: Go parseDBCMessage (json.go:1931-1965) validates id via NewStandardID/NewExtendedID and dlc via BytesToDLC; parseDBCSignal (2038-2051) rejects startBit<0||>511 and length<1||>64. C++ json_parse.cpp json_to_can_id (365-381) throws when standard id>0x7FF; parse_message_def (388-390) throws on bad bytes_to_dlc. (C++ start_bit/bit_length narrow-cast to uint16_t/uint8_t at 346-347 — rejects huge values but NOT the 512-65535 / 65-255 out-of-range band Go rejects, so C++ is partially permissive on those two.) Trigger re-verified: the public, documented MockBackend (_backend.py:544) replays arbitrary canned bytes; parse_dbc/parse_dbc_text route through parse_parsed_dbc_response->normalize_dbc (_response_parsers.py:153) and format_dbc->normalize_dbc (_client.py:453). A canned DBC response with id=9999 or dlc=7 is accepted by Python, rejected by Go/C++ decoders. No UB; mock-only conformance divergence.
suggested_fix: Add a `_normalize_message` helper paralleling `_normalize_value_entry`: validate `id` is an int within the standard (<=2047) or extended (<=0x1FFFFFFF) range per `extended`, validate `dlc` is a legal byte count (reuse the bytes->code table), require non-empty `name`; and in `normalize_signal` validate `startBit` in 0-511 and `length` in 1-64 — each raising `ProtocolError`, matching Go and the normalizer's existing varType/value-entry checks. Per the cross-binding consistency rule, also add the 512-65535 startBit / 65-255 length range rejection to C++ parse_signal_def so all three converge on Go's stricter (correct) direction.

title: Multiplexed-signal decode accepts empty or missing multiplex_values, where Go rejects both and C++ rejects missing
proof: Re-read normalize_signal (114-121): it copies the raw dict and converts only the four rational fields; it never inspects `multiplexor` or `multiplex_values`, so `{"multiplexor":"MUX","multiplex_values":[]}` (or with the key absent) passes through as a valid signal. Go parseSignalPresence (json.go:2128-2131) re-verified: `rawVals, ok := j["multiplex_values"].([]any); if !ok || len(rawVals) == 0 { return nil, protocolError("multiplex_values must be a non-empty array") }` — rejects BOTH missing and empty. C++ parse_signal_def (json_parse.cpp:315-323) re-verified: `if (j.contains("multiplexor")) { const auto& arr = j.at("multiplex_values"); ...` — `nlohmann::json::at` throws on missing key (rejects missing) but an empty array loops zero times producing Multiplexed{.multiplex_values={}} (accepts empty). Net three-way split exactly as claimed: Go rejects missing+empty, C++ rejects missing/accepts empty, Python accepts both. Trigger: same public-MockBackend parse_dbc/format_dbc path as finding 1. No UB.
suggested_fix: In normalize_signal, when the signal dict contains `multiplexor`, require `multiplex_values` to be a present non-empty list of ints and raise `ProtocolError` otherwise — matching Go. Per the cross-binding consistency rule the lockstep fix also touches C++ parse_signal_def (add the empty-array rejection); Go's stricter behavior is the correct convergence direction.

title: end_stream warning decode casts property_index with cast("int", w.get(...,0)) instead of validating, contradicting Python's own validate_integer_field used everywhere else and Go's strict check
proof: Re-read _streaming.py:550-558: warnings are built with `"property_index": cast("int", w.get("property_index", 0))` — `typing.cast` is a runtime no-op, so a float/string wire value is threaded into the typed CompleteWarning as a lying int (and surfaced to the ENDSTREAM_UNCACHED_ATOM log event at 562-569). The intra-Python inconsistency is real and verified: validate_integer_field (rational.py:114) IS used for the IDENTICAL `property_index` field at _response_parsers.py:233 (_parse_property_event) and :329 (parse_finalization_results). Go parseStreamResponse (json.go:1284-1286) is strict: `idx, err := parseNumberAsInt64(w["property_index"]); if err != nil { return nil, wrapProtocolError("warning property_index", err) }`. Refinement on the candidate's sub-claim: Go uses lenient getString for kind/detail (returns "" on non-string, does NOT raise) — the candidate's suggested_fix already acknowledges this, and the central property_index divergence is unaffected. Trigger: a `complete` response whose warning carries `"property_index": 1.5` is accepted by Python with a corrupted value where Go raises ProtocolError. Severity stays medium: this is the explicitly-forbidden typed-cast-lie pattern (build_error_response rationale, _response_parsers.py) on a value-bearing field.
suggested_fix: Replace the bare cast with `"property_index": validate_integer_field("property_index", w.get("property_index"))` (the helper already used by the response parsers); type-check `kind`/`detail` are str raising ProtocolError on mismatch (or, to match Go's getString leniency for those two, coerce defensively) — but at minimum stop casting a non-int into a typed int.

title: parse_extraction_buffer does not reject trailing bytes after the three binary segments, where Go rejects them as protocol drift
proof: Re-read _client_bin.py:184-187: `values, off = _parse_values_segment(...); errors, off = _parse_errors_segment(...); absent, _ = _parse_absent_segment(buf, off, nabss, names); return SignalExtractionResult(...)` — the final offset is discarded into `_` and there is NO `off != len(buf)` guard, so trailing bytes are silently ignored. Go parseExtractionBin (json.go:1163-1165) re-verified: `if off != len(buf) { return nil, protocolError(fmt.Sprintf("extraction binary buffer has %d trailing bytes", len(buf)-off)) }` with the rationale that extra bytes indicate Agda-writer/reader drift. The wire layout is fixed (AletheiaFFI.hs:269 — `Header(3xu16) + Values(x18B) + Errors(x3B) + Absent(x2B). Native byte order.`; candidate cited line 235, actual 269 — immaterial line drift), so a longer buffer is provable drift. C++ parse_extraction_bin (client.cpp:298) re-verified to guard only `buf.size() < expected_size`, not `>`, so Python matches C++ and only Go is strict — a Go-direction lockstep fix touching C++ too. No UB (over-read is impossible; the segments only consume what the header counts).
suggested_fix: After `_parse_absent_segment`, keep the returned offset: `absent, off = _parse_absent_segment(...)` then `if off != len(buf): raise ProtocolError(f"extraction binary buffer has {len(buf) - off} trailing bytes")`, matching Go. Apply the same `buf.size() > expected_size` rejection to C++ parse_extraction_bin (client.cpp:298) for cross-binding lockstep on Go's stricter direction.

title: Observed signal value in enriched_reason rendered via lossy %g float in Python/Go/C++, contradicting the exact formatℚ renderer used for the predicate side within the SAME string
lines: python _enrichment.py:410 (vs predicate at 226-236 via _format_rational); go enrich.go:263 + formatValue:152 (vs predicate at 128-145 via formatRational); cpp client.cpp:714 (vs enrich.cpp:32-33 via format_rational_ffi); rust enrich.rs:218 + check.rs:28-34 (counter)
proof:
RE-VERIFIED PERSONALLY. The defect is an INTERNAL inconsistency within each of Python/Go/C++: one enriched_reason string renders the predicate's ℚ via the exact Agda kernel formatℚ (aletheia_format_rational) but renders the observed ℚ via lossy float %g.

GROUND TRUTH (verified): src/Aletheia/CAN/Signal.agda:24-25 `SignalValue = ℚ`. haskell-shim/src/AletheiaFFI/BinaryOutput.hs:75-88 writeExtrValues emits `signal_index(u16) + numerator(i64) + denominator(i64)` per value — exact ℚ on the wire. src/Aletheia/DBC/RationalRenderer.agda:5-49 documents formatℚ as the cross-binding-identical human-readable renderer (`Rational 1 2 → 0.5`, `Rational 42 1 → 42`, exact decimal up to 18 places, else `n/d`).

INTERNAL INCONSISTENCY (verified at each render site):
- Python _enrichment.py: predicate side at 226-236 calls _format_rational → lib.aletheia_format_rational (195-219, the FFI kernel); observed side at 410 `parts.append(f"{sig} = {val:g}")` where val is a Fraction. EMPIRICAL: `{Fraction(1234567,1000):g}` → `1234.57` (6-sig-fig TRUNCATION, real precision loss; true value 1234.567); `{Fraction(1234567,1):g}` → `1.23457e+06` (scientific notation + truncation; formatℚ → `1234567`).
- Go enrich.go: predicate side at 128-145 via formatRational → formatRationalFFI (20-22, FFI kernel); observed side at 263 `formatValue(float64(val))`, formatValue at 152 = `fmt.Sprintf("%g", v)`. Additionally STRUCTURALLY lossy: types.go:28 `type PhysicalValue float64`, and json.go:1127-1131 collapses (num,den)→`float64(num)/float64(den)` at decode, so exactness is lost before render.
- C++ client.cpp:714 `std::format("{} = {:g}", ..., it->second.get().to_double())` for the observed value; predicate side via enrich.cpp:32-33 format_value(const Rational&) → detail::format_rational_ffi (the FFI kernel). PhysicalValue wraps an exact Rational (loss only at render). EMPIRICAL: `{:g}` on 1234.567 → `1234.57` (same truncation as Python).
- Rust (COUNTER-EXAMPLE) enrich.rs:218 renders observed via rat_str (check.rs:28-34: `n` or `n/d`, exact, never %g). Rust diverges in the OTHER direction: it is internally CONSISTENT but uses its own local rat_str on BOTH sides — enrich.rs:97-117 format_predicate also uses rat_str, NOT formatℚ. Rust calls aletheia_format_rational/formatℚ nowhere. check.rs:24-27 documents this as 'a documented display-only divergence from the peers' FFI decimals'. (NOTE: the original finding's claim that 'all four route the predicate through formatℚ' is FALSE for Rust — Rust uses rat_str on predicate AND observed; I corrected this. Rust is therefore not a formatℚ counter-example but an internally-consistent exact-but-non-canonical outlier.)

NOT GATED: tests pin only integer-valued observed values where %g and formatℚ coincide — cpp/tests/unit_tests_enrich.cpp:110/158/261 (`Speed = 245`, `RPM = 3000`, `Mode = 0`); python/tests/test_enrichment.py uses Fraction(245)/Fraction(3000). No test exercises a value like 1234.567 / 1234567, so the divergence is unpinned.
suggested_fix: DIRECTION CALL for the maintainer (do not fix unilaterally — per AGENTS.md line 31 case (c), consistent-but-wrong-by-principle against the canonical formatℚ spec). The clearest articulation: Python/Go/C++ each render TWO ℚ values in one enriched_reason string with two different renderers (predicate→exact formatℚ, observed→lossy %g) — an internal inconsistency, plus a precision loss / scientific-notation contradiction of the canonical renderer. Route the observed-value render through aletheia_format_rational in all three. Surface two asymmetries to the user: (1) Go is structurally lossy (PhysicalValue=float64, collapsed at json.go:1131) so its fix is larger — carry exact (num,den) through the extraction decode; Python (Fraction) and C++ (Rational) preserve exact and need only a one-line render swap. (2) Rust is a SEPARATE divergence: it renders both sides exactly but via its own rat_str (`1/2`) rather than formatℚ (`0.5`), so canonicalizing all four on formatℚ would also change Rust's output — fold into the same direction decision. Add a cross-binding test exercising 1234.567 / 1234567 to pin the chosen convention.
